Densities: The density problems consist of 34 probability density functions collected from scipy (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.norm.html).
Eponymous:
The Eponymous problems consist of formulas from Wikipedias "List of equations named after people".
The formulas have been scraped using the mistral-large LLM, which also suggested reasonable intervals for the variables.
We used the following prompt:
"From the following webpage, extract equations where a dependent variable is defined as a function of independent variables y = f(x). 
Any unknown function of a independent variable is a new independent variable and replaces the original variable. 
List these equations together with reasonable and finite value intervals for the independent variable and a one sentence description.
For each equation, output a json in the following format:
{
    'description' : one sentence description,
    'expression' : function in latex form with independent variables renamed x_0,...,x_k and the dependent variable renamed y
    'support' : list of finite example value intervals in the form of tuples [min, max] for each of the independent variables in python syntax. Avoid infinities.
    'scientific_field' : one of ['Physics', 'Biology', 'Mathematics', 'Chemistry', 'Engineering', 'Others'], which appears most fitting
}
Just output the jsons and NOTHING ELSE.
IMPORTANT: rename the independent variables as x_0,...,x_k and the dependent variable as y!".
Feynman:
The Feynman problems were introduced by Udresco et al. in their AIFeynman paper to test their symbolic regressor.
It has since been used and established by LaCava et al. in their comprehensive benchmark suite SRBench.
The database can be found at https://space.mit.edu/home/tegmark/aifeynman.html.
It consists of 117 problems split up into 97 simpler problems and 20 harder ('bonus') problems.
The simple problems are named after the volume and chapter they appear in the Feynman Lectures (https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/).
The harder problems were selected from
Classical Mechanics by Herbert Goldstein et al.,
Classical electrodynamics by Jackson et al.,
Gravitation and Cosmology: Principles and Applications of the General Theory of Relativity by Weinberg
Quantum Field Theory and the Standard Model by Matthew D. Schwartz.
Mystery problems from AIFeynman 2.0 have been sampled to get valid support intervals.
Keijzer:
The 15 Keijzer problems appear collectively the paper "Improving Symbolic Regression with Interval Arithmetic and Linear Scaling" by Maarten Keijzer. 
For Keijzer-9, which is actually arcsinh(x), we selected the alternative form log(x + sqrt(x^2 + 1)).
Korns:
The 15 Korns problems have been introduced by Michael Korns in his paper "Accuracy in Symbolic Regression", 
They were generated by a predefined grammar with shallow depth. We changed the variable name to only include valid variables.
Koza:
The Koza problems are two polynomial problems, that have been used in the seminal paper "Genetic Programming: On the Programming of Computers by Means of Natural Selection" by John Koza.
It was this paper that introduced the field of genetic programming. 
Note that "Koza-1" was omitted here, since it is the same as "Nguyen-2".
Livermore:
The Livermore Problems have been introduced by Mundhenk et al. in their paper "Symbolic Regression via Neural-Guided Genetic Programming Population Seeding".
Their goal was to design a benchmark problem set with a large range of problem difficulty. Note however, that also these problems are hand designed.
Nguyen:
The Nguyen problems have been introduced in the paper "Semantically-based crossover in genetic programming:
application to real-valued symbolic regression" by Nguyen et al.
They consist of 12 hand crafted functions, grouped into three types of functions: 
- polynomial functions
- trigonometric, logarithm and square-root functions
- bivariate functions
Corrections and extensions have been introduced in the paper "Genetic Programming Needs Better Benchmarks" by McDermott et al.
Pagie:
The Pagie problem has been used in the seminal paper "Evolutionary Consequences of Coevolving Targets" by Ludo Pagie and Paulien Hogeweg.
In the field of genetic programming, it is seen as especially difficult.
SRDS:
The SRDS problems have been created by Matsubara et al in the paper Rethinking Symbolic Regression Datasets and Benchmarks for Scientific Discovery.
Those problems are essentially the Feynman Problems with sample domains that reflect the underlying physical system.
Moreover, those problems are split into easy medium and hard problems. Also the issue of unused dummy variables was introduced.
Strogatz:
The Strogatz problems are a collection of 2D ordinary differential equations (ODEs) from Steven H. Strogatz book "Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos".
Framed as a regression problem, the goal is to predict the form of the ODE from the given gradients.
This problem collection was first introduced as a benchmark to symbolic regression in the SRBench testsuite.
The simulated time series and their gradients were taken from https://github.com/lacava/ode-strogatz.
Vladislavleva:
The Vladislavleva problems have been introduced by Ekaterina Vladislavleva et al. in their paper 
"Order of Nonlinearity as a Complexity Measure for Models Generated by Symbolic Regression via Pareto Genetic Programming", 
as nine especially difficult problems for classical genetic programming. 
Notably we use only eight problems, since the ninth problem, does not have a ground truth expression.
